review_text: To its credit, Lulu Wang’s The Farewell, despite the subject matter, resists the urge to dip into soppy sentimentality and avoids the temptation to engage in the overt manipulation of the audience. The premise: a family reunites in China to be with the matriarch of the family, who has been diagnosed with terminal lung cancer, a condition unbeknownst to the patient herself. The movie works mainly due to the interpersonal relationships within the family, making the familial quirks easy to relate to for most viewers, striking a nice balance between both humour and sorrow. On top of that, it all feels very natural thanks to the grounded performances and the realistic dialogue. If not spectacular, The Farewell is certainly well crafted throughout.

I went into this believing it was a comedy. It was marketed as a comedy, after all. While there were a couple of snicker-inducing moments, for the most part this was clearly a family drama. Poignant and full of excellent performances, I would have enjoyed this movie much more if I hadn't been waiting through the whole 90+ minutes for laughs that never came.

This film was great. Though it has been 5 years since this movie has released, I was amazed from the cinematography . The plot presented itself as a beauty of life in front of my eyes. The acting was stellar. It really felt like I was a part of the film. It was perfect even for an A24 movie.
Blend of comedy and drama with the right amount of each. Heartwarming and funny at the same time

Masterfully authentic to the Asian American experience without feeling too exclusionary. The more intimate moments are treated with dignity so as to feel relevant across borders and beliefs without sacrificing the Chinese essence of its source. Awkwafina was strong but, I thought Tzi Ma (father) and Diana Lin (mother) and Zhao Shu-zhen (Nai Nai / Grandmother) really did an amazing job of complementing and accenting the protagonist's story.

It was ok to learn about differences between American and Chinese culture if you don't know much about it. It shows people working through a difficult situation. There's little character development and it left me feeling like I got nothing much out of it.

A realistic drama of a Chinese family dealing with a grandmother seemingly soon to die. The toll it takes on the family, may sound boring but it's not, and Awkwifina(Nora). Provides an excellent acting performance.

3.25 STARS - Went to see The Farewell with my folks back in August 2019. Here I share my thoughts on the film. Video review: https://youtu.be/KmToUCX9MjQ ETA: The Farewell was my first ever reaction video. You can find it here if you want to check it out: https://youtu.be/5s1xohLWPFA
Dour, miserable characters. Hated it.
Balled my eyes out. Amazing movie
10/10 ! This movie is everything.
Give them the lobster they wanted the lobster they need the lobster her only grandson is getting married they deserve the lobster not the CRAB
Overrated and boring movie.
Beware of subtitles! This could have been an interesting movie, but the HBO version with subtitles for Mandarin was not really watchable. Barely a comedy.
a good film about family good setting and great acting a good a24 film
very nice liked much haha funny

Sentimental but ultimately too slow paced and dull to be worthy of a higher rating. Comparisons between East and West and philosophies of life are laid on a bit too thick but still in an Art-house manner. Never got into it and detested the pretentious finish. Watched on Film4.
Beautiful and deeply touching. A must see
Lulu Wang never over-explains the themes or specific cultural references, which allows the movie to wash over you. The ending is greatly moving in its simplicity.

My brother saw this alone in the theater and told me I had to watch it. I didn't get the chance until it started streaming. He was right--it's an incridible examination of how two cultures approach the same issue, both coming from a place of love. It is a moving film that may surprise you and will definitely make you think.

An interesting film to win a golden globe for best actress. A good look into Chinese culture that was worthy of my time. Awkwafina is really good and it is her best performance but the grandma is definitely the steal of the film as she has both the comedic talent and the acting to keep it going. Overall pretty good film but I would rather watch films with plots rather than watching films in culture as I have seen too many at the moment. Grade: A
Highly recommended. I'll definitely be rewatching soon.

This is a brilliant tale of family, culture, and tradition. The story lets you into the lives of this family that is all gathered together again. You see their pain, joy, and sorrow throughout with outstanding performances. It is also very well shot. The clips are intentional when a character just sits or stares, makes for some beautiful moments. I knew this film had high acclaim, and I see why. It graps a hold of you from the beginning and doesn't let go until the end.

A grounded story bridging generations, continents and mindsets. Seldomly a movie touches on themes as foreignness, affiliation, mourning, parting and reconnecting with such devotion and care. Certainly in line with 2019s strange cinema peak.

The touch and feel of the film is simple subtle and wonderful. Heartwarming. Poignant. It hit me in a similar place as "Lady Bird did." Specifically funny and touching about the sensitive moment all humans have to go through.
I was content throughout the entire movie and had a smile on my face almost the entire time. - positive

An incredibly personal film from a brilliant Lulu Wang, The Farewell instantly captures the hearts and minds of audiences around the globe. Shuzhen Zhao's captivating performance is heartwarming, endearing, and magnificently relatable. This film will remain an indie classic for years to come.
The best acting performance from Awkwafina!
This had gotten a lot of good ratings so I watched it. It was ok. Not a fan of subtitles and the ending was poor. But ok

A deeply moving ensemble piece, that is both hilarious and heartfelt. It wrestles with the idea of not knowing what to do that would best serve a loved one, especially when cultures and beliefs clash. The whole thing felt about as authentic as you can get.

I liked how understated this film is - it does well at portraying the quirks of various family members and showing how they all come together. Its relatively amusing at times, with some of the dialogue and at other times its quite thought provoking and poignant even. The subject matter/themes are quite serious and I'm pleased that their treated sensitively. It gives a good insight into Chinese family culture and how they deal with impending death. I would definitely recommend this film, yes.

A somber, quiet, introspective generational family drama about immigration that also successfully juggles tones of warmth, healing, acceptance, and love that touches the very core of a second-generation immigrant such as myself with split cultures and a bilingual tongue.
I found myself crying and appreciating the power of (my own) family

Advertised as a family drama comedy on Netflix, this (mostly subtitled) movie disappointed. It wasn't interesting to most of my family, it wasn't funny and the drama was dull as dishwater. At least it shows that Awkwafina has actually got some variety in her acting ability, though it didn't paint China in a nice light.
A wonderful film, but I can't believe anyone thinks it is a comedy (I mean, obviously someone does, but wow... what passes for comedy these days often seems much more like tragedy to me).

I'm sad most of the time, yet I'm occasionally amused. Although a thousand people's hearts contain a thousand Hamlets when it comes to the issue of culture and customs, I feel that everyone may find something to relate with in The Farewell's narrative.
Intimate, touching, and honest family drama with nary a false note.
Great movie! The shots are precise letting you see the movie from a far way prospective without losing the contact with the scene. A bit of a rushed ending but still good movie
A good movie about family and issues within each family.

Esta película es para el man que tiene una relación difícil con su abuelita. Para el que quiera recordar a su abuelita. Para el que es abuelita, para el que mató una abuelita... Bueno ya basta. Es para el que quiera recordar el valor de la familia y lo importante que es en nuestras vidas.
Wonderful, truly moving and a good reflection on family and grief. Thank you.

One of the rare instances of me aborting the watching of a movie. The premise of the story is very interesting, but it was mostly sub-titled Mandarin spoken language AND (more importantly) most of the sub-titles were white text on a white or light background. No English overdub available. Impossible to comprehend the dialogue and story.
This is an impressive debut by Wang! This was a beautiful story of cultural identity and family ties. It's great to see Awkwafina show off her acting skills in this heartwarming drama.
A poignantly told story based on a real lie, moving, ridiculous, hilarious, sad, an emotional rollercoaster. Great movie making.
reality that happens a lot in a family. from this I know that a sound mind will save man. Simple but beautiful story

Summary: Billi scrapes up the money to fly to China in order to say goodbye to her dying grandmother. Based on a true lie, the grandmother turns out to be the only family member that doesn't know she's ill. Takeaways: This movie amplifies the American-Chinese cross-cultural experience. Come along for family conflicts, a fake wedding scheme, and moral dilemmas.
i dont even know why its called The farewell when its just chineese
It makes you laugh, it makes you cry and it has an interesting plotline. What more could you ask for. The dialogue is superb and it makes you feel like you're listening to real conversations.
It's worth a watch but a little overrated. It is great that Hollywood is making movies of diversity and different cultures. Some of the writing just feels a little forced.
